title: High-Frequency vs. Low-Frequency Content
concept: Once an image is described in terms of frequency, its content sorts cleanly into two buckets: edges and fine texture live in the high-frequency range, while smooth gradients and flat backgrounds live in the low-frequency range.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the Fourier transform actually do to an image?

options.text: Re-describes the same picture as a combination of wave patterns rather than pixel values
Physically slices the image into two halves

exercises.explain: Right. Now that we can describe an image this way, let's look at exactly what ends up in the high vs. low frequency buckets.

cards.title: A mountain silhouette vs. hazy fog
cards.body: Look at a jagged mountain silhouette against the sky — the boundary snaps from dark rock to bright sky in an instant. Now look at morning fog rolling across a field — gray fades into gray so gradually you can't even point to where it 'starts.' One flips fast; one drifts slow.

cards.title: Edges and texture are high-frequency
cards.body: Anywhere brightness jumps sharply and repeatedly — a hard edge between two objects, or fine texture like fabric weave, gravel, or hair — falls into the high-frequency bucket. These are the fast, busy wiggles in an image's brightness.

cards.title: Roller coaster vs. gentle hill
cards.body: A roller coaster's track yanks you up and down again and again in seconds — that's the high-frequency stuff: edges and texture. A gentle grassy hill rises and falls once, slowly, over a long stretch — that's the low-frequency stuff: smooth gradients and flat backgrounds.

cards.title: One photo, two zones
cards.body: Take a portrait: the smooth, blurred-out background is almost entirely low-frequency, while the subject's hair, eyelashes, and the crisp outline of their face are packed with high-frequency detail. A single photo can contain both extremes side by side.

cards.title: It's not about size or zoom
cards.body: A huge smooth wall is still low-frequency even though it fills the whole frame, and a tiny patch of fine lace is still high-frequency even in a small corner of the photo. Frequency is about how fast brightness changes, not about size, zoom, or how important the object is.

cards.title: Low-frequency vs. high-frequency content
cards.body: Sorting real photo content into buckets:

left.text:
Smooth gradients, skies, plain backgrounds
Brightness drifts gradually

right.text:
Edges, texture, fine detail
Brightness jumps rapidly

exercises.prompt: Which pair correctly matches content type to frequency?

options.text: A sharp object outline = high-frequency; a smooth sky gradient = low-frequency
A sharp object outline = low-frequency; a smooth sky gradient = high-frequency
Both edges and smooth skies are always low-frequency
Frequency only applies to colorful images, not grayscale ones

exercises.explain: Correct — sharp, sudden brightness jumps are high-frequency, while gentle, gradual ones are low-frequency.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Fine texture, like the weave of a sweater, is generally high-frequency content.
exercises.explain: True. Fine texture packs in rapid brightness changes over a small area — the hallmark of high frequency.

exercises.sentence: A crisp edge between two objects causes brightness to jump ___, which places it in the high-frequency category.
exercises.explain: Sharply is correct — a hard, sudden brightness jump is exactly what makes an edge high-frequency.

exercises.prompt: In a portrait photo, which part is most likely dominated by LOW-frequency content?

options.text: The crisp outline of the subject's jawline

exercises.explain: Right — a soft, blurred background has almost no rapid brightness changes, making it low-frequency.

exercises.prompt: You're inspecting a photo of a forest. The distant smooth sky is low-frequency, and the dense, jumbled leaves and branches up close are high-frequency. Where would a foggy field in the far distance, where everything blurs together into gray, fall — and why?

options.text: Low-frequency — the brightness barely changes as fog smooths out all the sharp detail
It's undefined — fog has no frequency content at all
It depends only on the photo's file format

exercises.explain: Exactly! Fog smooths brightness into a slow, gentle drift — classic low-frequency. Now that we can sort content into these two buckets, here's the powerful part: we can deliberately keep or remove one bucket to control what a filter does.
